title: Campecarpus fulcitus
taxonomy: Campecarpus fulcitus is a species of plant in the family Apocynaceae. This family includes a wide variety of plants, such as periwinkle, oleander, and Madagascar periwinkle. The Apocynaceae family is part of the larger order Gentianales, which also includes families like Rubiaceae and Loganiaceae. The order Gentianales belongs to the clade Asterids, one of the two main groups within the angiosperm clade. Within the Asterids, the Gentianales order is part of the larger clade Euasterids I.

intro: Campecarpus fulcitus, also known as the "African Bloodwood," is a plant species that belongs to the family Apocynaceae, characterized by its unique red sap and ornamental value. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are believed to aid in wound healing and reduce fever. The bioactive constituents of Campecarpus fulcitus include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which have been shown to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. In traditional herbal medicine, Campecarpus fulcitus is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used to make tinctures and ointments, although its efficacy and safety have not been extensively scientifically validated.
